ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_00002473 (chicken noodle soup) is the closest parent; no specific Thai crispy chicken noodle soup entry exists in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (chicken soup with noodles) is the closest defensible parent category; no Thai-specific crispy chicken noodle soup code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept exists for Thai crispy chicken noodle soup as a specific food item.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Thai crispy chicken noodle soup weighing approximately 450 g, comprising crispy fried chicken pieces, springy noodles, and clear chicken broth with garnishes.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken noodle soup, Thai-style — estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data for chicken noodle soup and deep-fried chicken
nutrient_source.organization: FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Food Composition Table; USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Thai crispy chicken noodle soup exists in any approved primary source. Values are estimated by combining FAO/INFOODS SEA data for Thai chicken noodle soup (boiled noodles, chicken broth, chicken meat) with USDA SR Legacy data for deep-fried chicken skin-on to account for the crispy fried chicken component. Proportions estimated as approximately 35% noodles, 30% crispy fried chicken, 30% broth, 5% garnishes. Per-100g values derived from weighted average of components.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of noodle-based soups — analogous estimate based on egg noodles and rice noodles GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai crispy chicken noodle soup. GI estimated at 52 based on the dominant carbohydrate source being egg noodles or rice noodles. The University of Sydney GI Database reports GI of egg noodles at approximately 46–57 (mean ~52) and rice noodles at approximately 53–61. A blended estimate of 52 is used, reflecting the low-to-medium GI of the noodle base. The protein and fat content of the dish (chicken, broth) further attenuate the glycemic response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (52 × 56.25) / 100 ≈ 29.3, rounded to 29.5 for a 450 g serving containing approximately 56.25 g carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce, soy sauce, and chicken broth
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced-sodium broth, as a single 450 g bowl provides approximately 2,160 mg sodium, exceeding the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from noodle carbohydrates despite low-to-medium GI
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that a standard bowl provides approximately 56 g of carbohydrate with a glycemic load of ~29, which may require insulin or medication adjustment; choosing a smaller portion or requesting fewer noodles can help manage postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from deep-fried chicken skin
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should note the high sodium content and moderate saturated fat from the deep-fried chicken component; removing the chicken skin and requesting low-sodium broth can meaningfully reduce cardiovascular risk from this dish.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai Crispy Chicken Noodle Soup (ก๋วยเตี๋ยวไก่กรอบน้ำ, romanized: Kuai Tiao Kai Krob Nam) is a Thai noodle soup dish featuring deep-fried chicken pieces that are crispy on the outside and juicy on the inside, served in a clear, savoury chicken broth alongside springy egg or rice noodles. The dish is garnished with bean sprouts, spring onions, coriander, and sometimes fried garlic, and is seasoned with fish sauce and soy sauce. It is commonly served in soup style (nam) or dry style (haeng). The deep-frying of the chicken contributes a satisfying textural contrast between the crunchy coating and the tender meat, while the broth provides umami depth from long-simmered chicken bones and aromatics. Nutritionally, a standard 450 g bowl provides approximately 531 kcal, 35 g protein, 20 g fat, and 56 g carbohydrate. The dish is high in sodium (~2,160 mg per bowl) due to fish sauce and soy sauce, making it a concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at 52 (low to medium), but the glycemic load per serving is moderate at approximately 29, relevant for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is dairy-free and generally halal when prepared with halal-certified chicken. It is a popular street food and restaurant staple across Thailand and is widely available in Thai restaurants in Singapore.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Thai crispy chicken noodle soup exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, FAO/INFOODS SEA, USDA). Nutrient values are estimated from component-level data using FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA SR Legacy for analogous dishes and ingredients. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using the closest analogous noodle types. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Nutrient values are estimated from component analysis: approximately 35% egg/rice noodles, 30% deep-fried chicken (skin-on), 30% chicken broth, 5% garnishes (bean sprouts, spring onions, coriander). Per-100g values derived from weighted average of FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA SR Legacy component data. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the component source databases for this dish type. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the component source databases for this dish type. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the component source databases for this dish type. GI of 52 is an analogous estimate based on egg noodle and rice noodle GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database; the protein and fat content of the dish are expected to attenuate the glycemic response. Sodium content is notably high due to fish sauce and soy sauce seasoning; this is a primary clinical concern. The dish is served at Saap Saap Thai - Funan (Singapore) as menu item N6. Halal status assumed based on typical Thai restaurant practice in Singapore but not independently verified. The food_group is retained as 'Meat and alternatives' from the input record, reflecting the dominant protein component (deep-fried chicken), though the dish is a mixed noodle soup.
